About a 7-minute walk from the station. == As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here. == Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho. Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues. In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=}} and Nonaka Shrine. == Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746). The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall. == References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association] {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|和霊神社|version=99157836|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and Structures in Uwajima City]] [[Category:History of Uwajima City]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Warei Shrine]] [[en:Warei Shrine]] [[ja:和霊神社]] [[sn:arei Shrine]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:宇和島市の画像提供依頼]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:山家氏]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:歴史の画像提供依頼]] {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Warei Shrine |image=[[File:Warei Jinja Haiden.jpg|250px]]
main hall |location=[[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 |Rank degrees=33 | type minutes=13 | type seconds=47 | N/S=N |longitude degrees=132 |longitude minutes=33 |longitude seconds=55 | E/W=E |Country code=JP |map=Japan Ehime |Label position=left |deity={{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|lt=Yamaga Seibei Kiyomasa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} |Rank=Old {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|ja|近代Rank制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|qq=}} Prefectural Shrine, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] |founded=[[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Jōō]] 2 (1653) |Main hall= |Other names= |festival=[[:en:July 23|July 23]]・[[:en:July 24|July 24]] (Warei festival) |ceremony= }} {{ill|Warei Shrine|ja|和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine|qq=}} (和霊神社）is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]]. It is an old Prefectural Shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == deity == ;Main deity * {{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} (commonly known as Seibei) :Yamaga Kiyomasa was a retainer of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. In 1615 (Genna 1), he followed Masamune's eldest son, [[:en:Date Hidemune|Date Hidemune]], when he was transferred to Uwajima. As a chief retainer of the Uwajima Domain, he supported the domain's administration. Uwajima had been impoverished by the previous lord's misrule, but Kiyomasa implemented tax reductions and industrial promotion, which had a positive effect. However, in 1620 (Genna 6), Hidemune, influenced by slander from other retainers, believed the accusations and had Kiyomasa and his sons killed ({{ill|Warei Incident|ja|和霊騒動|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Incident|qq=}}). == History == The shrine is located at the northern end of Uwajima city, on the site of Kamegae Castle. The people who admired Kiyomasa secretly built a small shrine in the grounds of Yamen Koshin Shrine in Kitamori, where they enshrined the spirits of Kiyomasa and his family. Afterwards, those involved in Kiyomasa's murder began to die suddenly from lightning strikes, marine accidents, and other strange occurrences. When it became clear that Kiyomasa was innocent, Hidemune established a shrine dedicated to Kiyomasa in 1653 (Jōō 2) and named it Yamayorikawa Rei Shrine. As the number of worshippers increased and the shrine grounds became too small, the shrine was moved to Mukoyama (within the present {{ill|Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|ja|宇和島市立城北中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|qq=}}), then to Moriyasu. In 1731 (Kyōhō 16), the fourth domain lord {{ill|Date Muratoki|ja|伊達村年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Muratoki|qq=}} ordered the shrine to be moved again to a part of the Kamegae Castle ruins, and it was relocated to its present site in 1735 (Kyōhō 20). In 1919 (Taisho 8), the shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine. In 1953 (Showa 28), it was listed as a Special Shrine by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine was destroyed by air raids in 1945 (Showa 20) but was splendidly rebuilt in 1957 (Showa 32). Shrines enshrining the divided spirit of Warei Shrine can be found across Japan, mainly in the Shikoku and Chugoku regions. == Grounds == The shrine's forest was designated a Natural Monument by the city in 1974. == ceremony == On January 2nd and 3rd, there are offerings of iaijutsu (swordsmanship). The festival on July 23rd and 24th is called the Warei festival. == Local Information == ; location * [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 ; Transportation Access * Nearest station: [[:en:Uwajima Station|Uwajima Station]] on the [[:en:Yosan Line|Yosan Line]] operated by [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]]. About a 7-minute walk from the station. == As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here. == Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho. Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues. In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=}} and Nonaka Shrine. == Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746). The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall. == References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association] {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|和霊神社|version=99157836|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and Structures in Uwajima City]] [[Category:History of Uwajima City]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Warei Shrine]] [[ja:和霊神社]] [[sn:arei Shrine]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:宇和島市の画像提供依頼]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:山家氏]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:歴史の画像提供依頼]] {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Warei Shrine |image=[[File:Warei Jinja Haiden.jpg|250px]]
main hall |location=[[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 |Rank degrees=33 | type minutes=13 | type seconds=47 | N/S=N |longitude degrees=132 |longitude minutes=33 |longitude seconds=55 | E/W=E |Country code=JP |map=Japan Ehime |Label position=left |deity={{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|lt=Yamaga Seibei Kiyomasa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} |Rank=Old {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|ja|近代Rank制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Prefectural Shrine, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] |founded=[[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Jōō]] 2 (1653) |Main hall= |Other names= |festival=[[:en:July 23|July 23]]・[[:en:July 24|July 24]] (Warei festival) |ceremony= }} {{ill|Warei Shrine|ja|和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine|qq=}} (和霊神社）is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]]. It is an old Prefectural Shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == deity == ;Main deity * {{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} (commonly known as Seibei) :Yamaga Kiyomasa was a retainer of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. In 1615 (Genna 1), he followed Masamune's eldest son, [[:en:Date Hidemune|Date Hidemune]], when he was transferred to Uwajima. As a chief retainer of the Uwajima Domain, he supported the domain's administration. Uwajima had been impoverished by the previous lord's misrule, but Kiyomasa implemented tax reductions and industrial promotion, which had a positive effect. However, in 1620 (Genna 6), Hidemune, influenced by slander from other retainers, believed the accusations and had Kiyomasa and his sons killed ({{ill|Warei Incident|ja|和霊騒動|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Incident|qq=}}). == History == The shrine is located at the northern end of Uwajima city, on the site of Kamegae Castle. The people who admired Kiyomasa secretly built a small shrine in the grounds of Yamen Koshin Shrine in Kitamori, where they enshrined the spirits of Kiyomasa and his family. Afterwards, those involved in Kiyomasa's murder began to die suddenly from lightning strikes, marine accidents, and other strange occurrences. When it became clear that Kiyomasa was innocent, Hidemune established a shrine dedicated to Kiyomasa in 1653 (Jōō 2) and named it Yamayorikawa Rei Shrine. As the number of worshippers increased and the shrine grounds became too small, the shrine was moved to Mukoyama (within the present {{ill|Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|ja|宇和島市立城北中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|qq=}}), then to Moriyasu. In 1731 (Kyōhō 16), the fourth domain lord {{ill|Date Muratoki|ja|伊達村年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Muratoki|qq=}} ordered the shrine to be moved again to a part of the Kamegae Castle ruins, and it was relocated to its present site in 1735 (Kyōhō 20). In 1919 (Taisho 8), the shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine. In 1953 (Showa 28), it was listed as a Special Shrine by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine was destroyed by air raids in 1945 (Showa 20) but was splendidly rebuilt in 1957 (Showa 32). Shrines enshrining the divided spirit of Warei Shrine can be found across Japan, mainly in the Shikoku and Chugoku regions. == Grounds == The shrine's forest was designated a Natural Monument by the city in 1974. == ceremony == On January 2nd and 3rd, there are offerings of iaijutsu (swordsmanship). The festival on July 23rd and 24th is called the Warei festival. == Local Information == ; location * [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 ; Transportation Access * Nearest station: [[:en:Uwajima Station|Uwajima Station]] on the [[:en:Yosan Line|Yosan Line]] operated by [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]]. About a 7-minute walk from the station. == As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here. == Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho. Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues. In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nonaka Shrine. == Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746). The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall. == References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association] {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|和霊神社|version=99157836|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and Structures in Uwajima City]] [[Category:History of Uwajima City]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Warei Shrine]] [[ja:和霊神社]] [[sn:arei Shrine]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:宇和島市の画像提供依頼]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:山家氏]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:歴史の画像提供依頼]] {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall. == References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association] {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|和霊神社|version=99157836|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Warei Shrine]] [[ja:和霊神社]] [[sn:arei Shrine]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:宇和島市の画像提供依頼]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:山家氏]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:歴史の画像提供依頼]] {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Warei Shrine |image=[[File:Warei Jinja Haiden.jpg|250px]]
main hall |location=[[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 |Rank degrees=33 | type minutes=13 | type seconds=47 | N/S=N |longitude degrees=132 |longitude minutes=33 |longitude seconds=55 | E/W=E |Country code=JP |map=Japan Ehime |Label position=left |deity={{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|lt=Yamaga Seibei Kiyomasa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} |Rank=Old {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|ja|近代Rank制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Prefectural Shrine, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] |founded=[[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Jōō]] 2 (1653) |Main hall= |Other names= |festival=[[:en:July 23|July 23]]・[[:en:July 24|July 24]] (Warei festival) |ceremony= }} {{ill|Warei Shrine|ja|和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine|qq=}} (和霊神社）is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]]. It is an old Prefectural Shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == deity == ;Main deity * {{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} (commonly known as Seibei) :Yamaga Kiyomasa was a retainer of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. In 1615 (Genna 1), he followed Masamune's eldest son, [[:en:Date Hidemune|Date Hidemune]], when he was transferred to Uwajima. As a chief retainer of the Uwajima Domain, he supported the domain's administration. Uwajima had been impoverished by the previous lord's misrule, but Kiyomasa implemented tax reductions and industrial promotion, which had a positive effect. However, in 1620 (Genna 6), Hidemune, influenced by slander from other retainers, believed the accusations and had Kiyomasa and his sons killed ({{ill|Warei Incident|ja|和霊騒動|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Incident|qq=}}). == History == The shrine is located at the northern end of Uwajima city, on the site of Kamegae Castle. The people who admired Kiyomasa secretly built a small shrine in the grounds of Yamen Koshin Shrine in Kitamori, where they enshrined the spirits of Kiyomasa and his family. Afterwards, those involved in Kiyomasa's murder began to die suddenly from lightning strikes, marine accidents, and other strange occurrences. When it became clear that Kiyomasa was innocent, Hidemune established a shrine dedicated to Kiyomasa in 1653 (Jōō 2) and named it Yamayorikawa Rei Shrine. As the number of worshippers increased and the shrine grounds became too small, the shrine was moved to Mukoyama (within the present {{ill|Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|ja|宇和島市立城北中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|qq=}}), then to Moriyasu. In 1731 (Kyōhō 16), the fourth domain lord {{ill|Date Muratoki|ja|伊達村年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Muratoki|qq=}} ordered the shrine to be moved again to a part of the Kamegae Castle ruins, and it was relocated to its present site in 1735 (Kyōhō 20). In 1919 (Taisho 8), the shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine. In 1953 (Showa 28), it was listed as a Special Shrine by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine was destroyed by air raids in 1945 (Showa 20) but was splendidly rebuilt in 1957 (Showa 32). Shrines enshrining the divided spirit of Warei Shrine can be found across Japan, mainly in the Shikoku and Chugoku regions. == Grounds == The shrine's forest was designated a Natural Monument by the city in 1974. == ceremony == On January 2nd and 3rd, there are offerings of iaijutsu (swordsmanship). The festival on July 23rd and 24th is called the Warei festival. == Local Information == ; location * [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 ; Transportation Access * Nearest station: [[:en:Uwajima Station|Uwajima Station]] on the [[:en:Yosan Line|Yosan Line]] operated by [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]]. About a 7-minute walk from the station. == As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here. == Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho. Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues. In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nonaka Shrine. == Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746). The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall. == References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association] {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|和霊神社|version=99157836|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Warei Shrine]] [[ja:和霊神社]] [[sn:arei Shrine]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:宇和島市の画像提供依頼]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:山家氏]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:歴史の画像提供依頼]] {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

In 1919 (Taisho 8), the shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine. In 1953 (Showa 28), it was listed as a Special Shrine by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine was destroyed by air raids in 1945 (Showa 20) but was splendidly rebuilt in 1957 (Showa 32). Shrines enshrining the divided spirit of Warei Shrine can be found across Japan, mainly in the Shikoku and Chugoku regions. == Grounds == The shrine's forest was designated a Natural Monument by the city in 1974. == ceremony == On January 2nd and 3rd, there are offerings of iaijutsu (swordsmanship). The festival on July 23rd and 24th is called the Warei festival. == Local Information == ; location * [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 ; Transportation Access * Nearest station: [[:en:Uwajima Station|Uwajima Station]] on the [[:en:Yosan Line|Yosan Line]] operated by [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]]. About a 7-minute walk from the station. == As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here. == Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho. Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues. In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nonaka Shrine. == Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746). The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall. == References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association] {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|和霊神社|version=99157836|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Warei Shrine]] [[ja:和霊神社]] [[sn:arei Shrine]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:宇和島市の Image Requests]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:山家氏]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Image Requests on History]] {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Warei Shrine |image=[[File:Warei Jinja Haiden.jpg|250px]]
main hall |location=[[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 |Rank degrees=33 | type minutes=13 | type seconds=47 | N/S=N |longitude degrees=132 |longitude minutes=33 |longitude seconds=55 | E/W=E |Country code=JP |map=Japan Ehime |Label position=left |deity={{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|lt=Yamaga Seibei Kiyomasa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} |Rank=Old {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|ja|近代Rank制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Prefectural Shrine, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] |founded=[[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Jōō]] 2 (1653) |Main hall= |Other names= |festival=[[:en:July 23|July 23]]・[[:en:July 24|July 24]] (Warei festival) |ceremony= }} {{ill|Warei Shrine|ja|和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine|qq=}} (和霊神社）is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]]. It is an old Prefectural Shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == deity == ;Main deity * {{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} (commonly known as Seibei) :Yamaga Kiyomasa was a retainer of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. In 1615 (Genna 1), he followed Masamune's eldest son, [[:en:Date Hidemune|Date Hidemune]], when he was transferred to Uwajima. As a chief retainer of the Uwajima Domain, he supported the domain's administration. Uwajima had been impoverished by the previous lord's misrule, but Kiyomasa implemented tax reductions and industrial promotion, which had a positive effect. However, in 1620 (Genna 6), Hidemune, influenced by slander from other retainers, believed the accusations and had Kiyomasa and his sons killed ({{ill|Warei Incident|ja|和霊騒動|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Incident|qq=}}). == History == The shrine is located at the northern end of Uwajima city, on the site of Kamegae Castle. The people who admired Kiyomasa secretly built a small shrine in the grounds of Yamen Koshin Shrine in Kitamori, where they enshrined the spirits of Kiyomasa and his family. Afterwards, those involved in Kiyomasa's murder began to die suddenly from lightning strikes, marine accidents, and other strange occurrences. When it became clear that Kiyomasa was innocent, Hidemune established a shrine dedicated to Kiyomasa in 1653 (Jōō 2) and named it Yamayorikawa Rei Shrine. As the number of worshippers increased and the shrine grounds became too small, the shrine was moved to Mukoyama (within the present {{ill|Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|ja|宇和島市立城北中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|qq=}}), then to Moriyasu. In 1731 (Kyōhō 16), the fourth domain lord {{ill|Date Muratoki|ja|伊達村年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Muratoki|qq=}} ordered the shrine to be moved again to a part of the Kamegae Castle ruins, and it was relocated to its present site in 1735 (Kyōhō 20). In 1919 (Taisho 8), the shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine. In 1953 (Showa 28), it was listed as a Special Shrine by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine was destroyed by air raids in 1945 (Showa 20) but was splendidly rebuilt in 1957 (Showa 32). Shrines enshrining the divided spirit of Warei Shrine can be found across Japan, mainly in the Shikoku and Chugoku regions. == Grounds == The shrine's forest was designated a Natural Monument by the city in 1974. == ceremony == On January 2nd and 3rd, there are offerings of iaijutsu (swordsmanship). The festival on July 23rd and 24th is called the Warei festival. == Local Information == ; location * [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 ; Transportation Access * Nearest station: [[:en:Uwajima Station|Uwajima Station]] on the [[:en:Yosan Line|Yosan Line]] operated by [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]]. About a 7-minute walk from the station. == As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here. == Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho. Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues. In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nonaka Shrine. == Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746). The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall. == References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association] {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|和霊神社|version=99157836|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Warei Shrine]] [[ja:和霊神社]] [[sn:arei Shrine]] [[Category:区市町村指定天然記念物]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:宇和島市の Image Requests]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:Yamaga Clan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Image Requests on History]] {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Warei Shrine |image=[[File:Warei Jinja Haiden.jpg|250px]]
main hall |location=[[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 |Rank degrees=33 | type minutes=13 | type seconds=47 | N/S=N |longitude degrees=132 |longitude minutes=33 |longitude seconds=55 | E/W=E |Country code=JP |map=Japan Ehime |Label position=left |deity={{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|lt=Yamaga Seibei Kiyomasa|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} |Rank=Old {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|ja|近代Rank制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Prefectural Shrine, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] |founded=[[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Jōō]] 2 (1653) |Main hall= |Other names= |festival=[[:en:July 23|July 23]]・[[:en:July 24|July 24]] (Warei festival) |ceremony= }} {{ill|Warei Shrine|ja|和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine|qq=}} (和霊神社）is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]]. It is an old Prefectural Shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system. == deity == ;Main deity * {{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=}} (commonly known as Seibei) :Yamaga Kiyomasa was a retainer of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. In 1615 (Genna 1), he followed Masamune's eldest son, [[:en:Date Hidemune|Date Hidemune]], when he was transferred to Uwajima. As a chief retainer of the Uwajima Domain, he supported the domain's administration. Uwajima had been impoverished by the previous lord's misrule, but Kiyomasa implemented tax reductions and industrial promotion, which had a positive effect. However, in 1620 (Genna 6), Hidemune, influenced by slander from other retainers, believed the accusations and had Kiyomasa and his sons killed ({{ill|Warei Incident|ja|和霊騒動|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Incident|qq=}}). == History == The shrine is located at the northern end of Uwajima city, on the site of Kamegae Castle. The people who admired Kiyomasa secretly built a small shrine in the grounds of Yamen Koshin Shrine in Kitamori, where they enshrined the spirits of Kiyomasa and his family. Afterwards, those involved in Kiyomasa's murder began to die suddenly from lightning strikes, marine accidents, and other strange occurrences. When it became clear that Kiyomasa was innocent, Hidemune established a shrine dedicated to Kiyomasa in 1653 (Jōō 2) and named it Yamayorikawa Rei Shrine. As the number of worshippers increased and the shrine grounds became too small, the shrine was moved to Mukoyama (within the present {{ill|Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|ja|宇和島市立城北中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|qq=}}), then to Moriyasu. In 1731 (Kyōhō 16), the fourth domain lord {{ill|Date Muratoki|ja|伊達村年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Muratoki|qq=}} ordered the shrine to be moved again to a part of the Kamegae Castle ruins, and it was relocated to its present site in 1735 (Kyōhō 20). In 1919 (Taisho 8), the shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine. In 1953 (Showa 28), it was listed as a Special Shrine by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine was destroyed by air raids in 1945 (Showa 20) but was splendidly rebuilt in 1957 (Showa 32). Shrines enshrining the divided spirit of Warei Shrine can be found across Japan, mainly in the Shikoku and Chugoku regions. == Grounds == The shrine's forest was designated a Natural Monument by the city in 1974. == ceremony == On January 2nd and 3rd, there are offerings of iaijutsu (swordsmanship). The festival on July 23rd and 24th is called the Warei festival. == Local Information == ; location * [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 ; Transportation Access * Nearest station: [[:en:Uwajima Station|Uwajima Station]] on the [[:en:Yosan Line|Yosan Line]] operated by [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]]. About a 7-minute walk from the station. == As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here. == Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho. Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues. In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nonaka Shrine. == Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746). The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall. == References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}} === Sources === {{Reflist}} == External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association] {{shinto-stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|和霊神社|version=99157836|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Warei Shrine]] [[ja:和霊神社]] [[sn:arei Shrine]] [[Category:Ward and municipal designated natural monuments]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:Image requests in Uwajima City]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:Yamaga Clan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Image Requests on History]] {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

== Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746).
The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall.

<!--Warei Shrine--><!--Warei Shrine--><!--Warei Shrine--><!--Warei Shrine--><!--Warei Shrine--><!--Warei Shrine--> [[Category:Ward and municipal designated natural monuments]] [[Category:Buildings in Uwajima, Ehime]] [[Category:History of Uwajima]] [[Category:Image requests in Uwajima City]] [[Category:Uwajima Domain]] [[Category:Yamaga Clan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ehime Prefecture]] [[Category:Image Requests on History]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Warei-jinja}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11418237]] Wikidata name: Warei-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Warei Shrine |image=File:Warei Jinja Haiden.jpg |location=[[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 |Rank degrees=33 | type minutes=13 | type seconds=47 | N/S=N |longitude degrees=132 |longitude minutes=33 |longitude seconds=55 | E/W=E |Country code=JP |map=Japan Ehime |Label position=left |deity={{ill|Yanbe Kinmochi|qid=Q11467754}} |Rank=Old {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|ja|近代Rank制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Prefectural Shrine, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] |founded=[[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Jōō]] 2 (1653) |Main hall= |Other names= |festival=[[:en:July 23|July 23]]・[[:en:July 24|July 24]] (Warei festival) |ceremony= |prefecture={{ill|Uwajima|qid=Q845857}} |coordinates={{Coord|33.22972222222222|N|132.56527777777777|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{ill|Warei Shrine|ja|和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine|qq=}} (和霊神社）is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]]. It is an old Prefectural Shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == ;Main deity * {{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=|1=Yanbe Kinmochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11467754}} (commonly known as Seibei) :Yamaga Kiyomasa was a retainer of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. In 1615 (Genna 1), he followed Masamune's eldest son, [[:en:Date Hidemune|Date Hidemune]], when he was transferred to Uwajima. As a chief retainer of the Uwajima Domain, he supported the domain's administration. Uwajima had been impoverished by the previous lord's misrule, but Kiyomasa implemented tax reductions and industrial promotion, which had a positive effect. However, in 1620 (Genna 6), Hidemune, influenced by slander from other retainers, believed the accusations and had Kiyomasa and his sons killed ({{ill|Warei Incident|ja|和霊騒動|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Incident|qq=|1=Warei Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418238}}).
== History == The shrine is located at the northern end of Uwajima city, on the site of Kamegae Castle. The people who admired Kiyomasa secretly built a small shrine in the grounds of Yamen Koshin Shrine in Kitamori, where they enshrined the spirits of Kiyomasa and his family. Afterwards, those involved in Kiyomasa's murder began to die suddenly from lightning strikes, marine accidents, and other strange occurrences.
When it became clear that Kiyomasa was innocent, Hidemune established a shrine dedicated to Kiyomasa in 1653 (Jōō 2) and named it Yamayorikawa Rei Shrine. As the number of worshippers increased and the shrine grounds became too small, the shrine was moved to Mukoyama (within the present {{ill|Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|ja|宇和島市立城北中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|qq=|1=Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449134}}), then to Moriyasu. In 1731 (Kyōhō 16), the fourth domain lord {{ill|Date Muratoki|ja|伊達村年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Muratoki|qq=|1=Date Muratoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885152}} ordered the shrine to be moved again to a part of the Kamegae Castle ruins, and it was relocated to its present site in 1735 (Kyōhō 20). In 1919 (Taisho 8), the shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine. In 1953 (Showa 28), it was listed as a Special Shrine by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine was destroyed by air raids in 1945 (Showa 20) but was splendidly rebuilt in 1957 (Showa 32). Shrines enshrining the divided spirit of Warei Shrine can be found across Japan, mainly in the Shikoku and Chugoku regions.
== Grounds == The shrine's forest was designated a Natural Monument by the city in 1974.
== ceremony == On January 2nd and 3rd, there are offerings of iaijutsu (swordsmanship). The festival on July 23rd and 24th is called the Warei festival.
== Local Information == ; location * [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451
; Transportation Access * Nearest station: [[:en:Uwajima Station|Uwajima Station]] on the [[:en:Yosan Line|Yosan Line]] operated by [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]]. About a 7-minute walk from the station.
== As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=|1=Wolf Fire of the South Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q120335195}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here.
== Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho.
Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues.
In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=|1=Sendai Forus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377761}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nonaka Shrine.
== Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746).
The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=|1=Marunouchi Warei Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11367983}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=|1=Torao Akashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20038410}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11418237]] Wikidata name: Warei-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Warei Shrine |image=File:Warei Jinja Haiden.jpg |location=[[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451 |Rank degrees=33 | type minutes=13 | type seconds=47 | N/S=N |longitude degrees=132 |longitude minutes=33 |longitude seconds=55 | E/W=E |Country code=JP |map=Japan Ehime |Label position=left |deity={{ill|Yanbe Kinmochi|qid=Q11467754}} |Rank=Old {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|ja|近代Rank制度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Prefectural Shrine, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] |founded=[[:en:Jōō (Edo period)|Jōō]] 2 (1653) |Main hall= |Other names= |festival=[[:en:July 23|July 23]]・[[:en:July 24|July 24]] (Warei festival) |ceremony= |prefecture={{ill|Uwajima|qid=Q845857}} |coordinates={{Coord|33.22972222222222|N|132.56527777777777|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{ill|Warei Shrine|ja|和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine|qq=}} (和霊神社）is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]]. It is an old Prefectural Shrine under the modern shrine Ranking system.
== deity == ;Main deity * {{ill|Yamaga Kiyomasa|ja|山家公頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaga Kiyomasa|qq=|1=Yanbe Kinmochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11467754}} (commonly known as Seibei) :Yamaga Kiyomasa was a retainer of [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]]. In 1615 (Genna 1), he followed Masamune's eldest son, [[:en:Date Hidemune|Date Hidemune]], when he was transferred to Uwajima. As a chief retainer of the Uwajima Domain, he supported the domain's administration. Uwajima had been impoverished by the previous lord's misrule, but Kiyomasa implemented tax reductions and industrial promotion, which had a positive effect. However, in 1620 (Genna 6), Hidemune, influenced by slander from other retainers, believed the accusations and had Kiyomasa and his sons killed ({{ill|Warei Incident|ja|和霊騒動|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Incident|qq=|1=Warei Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418238}}).
== History == The shrine is located at the northern end of Uwajima city, on the site of Kamegae Castle. The people who admired Kiyomasa secretly built a small shrine in the grounds of Yamen Koshin Shrine in Kitamori, where they enshrined the spirits of Kiyomasa and his family. Afterwards, those involved in Kiyomasa's murder began to die suddenly from lightning strikes, marine accidents, and other strange occurrences.
When it became clear that Kiyomasa was innocent, Hidemune established a shrine dedicated to Kiyomasa in 1653 (Jōō 2) and named it Yamayorikawa Rei Shrine. As the number of worshippers increased and the shrine grounds became too small, the shrine was moved to Mukoyama (within the present {{ill|Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|ja|宇和島市立城北中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|qq=|1=Uwajima City Jonan Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11449134}}), then to Moriyasu. In 1731 (Kyōhō 16), the fourth domain lord {{ill|Date Muratoki|ja|伊達村年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Muratoki|qq=|1=Date Muratoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10885152}} ordered the shrine to be moved again to a part of the Kamegae Castle ruins, and it was relocated to its present site in 1735 (Kyōhō 20). In 1919 (Taisho 8), the shrine was designated a Prefectural Shrine. In 1953 (Showa 28), it was listed as a Special Shrine by the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]].
The shrine was destroyed by air raids in 1945 (Showa 20) but was splendidly rebuilt in 1957 (Showa 32). Shrines enshrining the divided spirit of Warei Shrine can be found across Japan, mainly in the Shikoku and Chugoku regions.
== Grounds == The shrine's forest was designated a Natural Monument by the city in 1974.
== ceremony == On January 2nd and 3rd, there are offerings of iaijutsu (swordsmanship). The festival on July 23rd and 24th is called the Warei festival.
== Local Information == ; location * [[:en:Ehime Prefecture|Ehime Prefecture]], [[:en:Uwajima, Ehime|Uwajima]], Warei Town 1451
; Transportation Access * Nearest station: [[:en:Uwajima Station|Uwajima Station]] on the [[:en:Yosan Line|Yosan Line]] operated by [[:en:Shikoku Railway Company|JR Shikoku]]. About a 7-minute walk from the station.
== As a Filming location == * The movie "{{ill|Wolf Fire of the South Sea|ja|南海の狼火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wolf Fire of the South Sea|qq=|1=Wolf Fire of the South Sea|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q120335195}}" (1960, Nikkatsu), starring [[:en:Akira Kobayashi|Akira Kobayashi]] was filmed here.
== Connection with Sendai == The descendants of Kiyomasa's eldest son, Kiyobei, who remained in Sendai, include Yamataka Toyozaburo (Yorimichi), who enshrined the divided spirit from the Warei Shrine in Uwajima as "Yamataka Myojin" in his residence on Higashi Ichibancho.
Later, "Yamataka Myojin" was renamed "Warei Shrine", and in 1883 (Meiji 16), the shrine was relocated to the premises at the corner of Higashi Nibancho and Tatemachi Streets. During the air raid on Sendai in July 1945 (Showa 20), the shrine was completely burned down, leaving only two guardian dog statues.
In 1973 (Showa 48), the shrine was relocated to Taihaku (present {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|ja|青葉区台原|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aoba-ku, Sendai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) along with the relocation of the Yamataka family. In 1977 (Showa 52), following the demand from Ichibancho shopping street, which had previously hosted the "Yamataka Myojin", the shrine was re-enshrined on the roof of the present-day {{ill|Sendai Forus|ja|仙台フォーラス|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Forus|qq=|1=Sendai Forus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377761}} shopping complex. Every July, as one of the "Ichibancho Three Shrines festival," the mikoshi of Warei Shrine parades through the central shopping streets of Sendai along with those of the {{ill|Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|ja|藤崎えびす神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisaki Ebisu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Nonaka Shrine.
== Warei Faith == Worshipped mainly in the Chugoku and Shikoku regions, as of 1982, there are 159 branch shrines across 9 prefectures in western Japan (including 82 in Ehime Prefecture and 26 in Okayama Prefecture) and one in the Tohoku region (two in Sendai City). The earliest example of the worship being introduced is the Warei Shrine in Rokutanji, Shimanto Town, Kochi Prefecture (1737), and Warei Shrine in Hokaiji Temple in Tamagawa Town, Imabari City, Ehime Prefecture (1746).
The former is believed to be the place where Kiyomasa's wife Sumi died, and both Kiyomasa and his wife Sumi are enshrined there. The latter shrine was established following a divine message suggesting worship at Warei Shrine to cure illness, with local landlord Yokari Yomemon becoming a worshipper after his illness was completely cured. Initially, it was an auxiliary shrine within Mishima Shrine (a branch of Oyamazumi Shrine), but as the number of worshippers increased, it became an independent shrine.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|ja|和霊神社 (高知市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warei Shrine (Kochi City)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - A shrine in Kochi City, Kochi Prefecture. The fourth-generation ancestor of [[:en:Sakamoto Ryōma|Sakamoto Ryoma]] enshrined the spirit from Warei Shrine. * {{ill|Marunouchi Warei Shrine|ja|丸之内和霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Marunouchi Warei Shrine|qq=|1=Marunouchi Warei Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11367983}} - A shrine on the site of Kiyomasa's residence. * {{ill|Akio Toru|ja|明石虎雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akio Toru|qq=|1=Torao Akashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20038410}} - A local architect from Uwajima who designed, supervised, and constructed the shrine's worship hall.
== References == === Annotations === {{Reflist|group="annotations"}}
=== Sources === {{Reflist}}
== External Links == {{commons|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Warei-jinja}} * [http://www.uwajima.org/spot/index3.html Uwajima City Tourism Association (Warei Shrine)] * [http://ehime-jinjacho.jp/jinja/?p=444 Warei Shrine - Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association]
